concepts.lesson: Food security means availability, accessibility, and affordability of food to all people at all times. It is ensured only if there is enough food for everyone, everyone has the capacity to buy it, and there is no barrier to access.
concepts.real_example: During a natural calamity like a drought, total production of food grains decreases, leading to a shortage and price rise.
key_points: Availability: Food production within the country plus imports and stock.
Accessibility: Food is within reach of every person without discrimination.
Affordability: Individuals have enough money to buy sufficient, safe, and nutritious food.

concepts.lesson: A large section of people suffer from food and nutrition insecurity in India. The worst affected are landless people, traditional artisans, petty self-employed workers, and the destitute. In urban areas, it is those in low-paid occupations and casual labor.
concepts.real_example: The social composition, along with the inability to buy food, plays a role. SCs, STs, and some sections of OBCs are more prone to food insecurity.
key_points: Pregnant and nursing mothers and children under 5 are a highly vulnerable group.
Regional disparities exist: states like Bihar, Odisha, and parts of UP face higher insecurity.
Hunger is not just an expression of poverty; it brings about poverty.

concepts.concept_name: Types of Hunger: Seasonal and Chronic
concepts.lesson: Hunger has two aspects: Chronic and Seasonal. Chronic hunger is a consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity and quality. Seasonal hunger is related to cycles of food growing and harvesting.
concepts.real_example: A casual construction laborer in a city may face seasonal hunger when there is no work during the rainy season.
key_points: Chronic hunger is caused by very low income and inability to buy food for survival.
Seasonal hunger is prevalent in rural areas due to the seasonal nature of agriculture.
India is aiming at Self-Sufficiency in food grains to eliminate both types.

concepts.lesson: Buffer Stock is the stock of food grains, namely wheat and rice, procured by the government through the Food Corporation of India (FCI). This is done to distribute food in deficit areas and among poorer strata at lower prices.
concepts.real_example: FCI purchases wheat and rice from farmers in states where there is surplus production at a pre-announced Minimum Support Price (MSP).
key_points: MSP is the price at which the government purchases crops to incentivize farmers.
Issue Price is the lower price at which the government sells grain to the poor.
Buffer stock helps resolve the problem of shortage during adverse weather conditions.

concepts.lesson: The food procured by the FCI is distributed through government-regulated ration shops, also known as Fair Price Shops. This system is the most important step taken by the Government of India towards ensuring food security.
concepts.real_example: There are about 5.5 lakh ration shops all over the country selling items like kerosene, sugar, and food grains at subsidized rates.
key_points: Targeted PDS (TPDS) was introduced in 1997 to focus on the poor.
Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) targets the 'poorest of the poor'.
PDS has faced criticism for 'leakages' and diversion of grains to the open market.

concepts.concept_name: Role of Cooperatives in Food Security
concepts.lesson: The cooperatives are playing an important role in food security in India, especially in the southern and western parts of the country. They set up shops to sell controlled goods at reasonable prices.
concepts.real_example: Mother Dairy in Delhi provides milk and vegetables to consumers at controlled rates decided by the Government of Delhi.
key_points: Amul is a success story of cooperatives in milk and milk products from Gujarat.
Grain banks have been set up by NGOs like ADS in Maharashtra for tribal people.
Cooperatives provide an alternative to government-run ration shops.
